topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box sat on my worn kitchen table, an incongruous splash of vibrant color against the muted tones of my apartment. Inside, the roses were impossibly perfect, their petals thick and velvety, a shocking crimson that seemed to absorb the weak afternoon light. There was no note. Just the flowers, nestled in crinkled tissue paper. My hands, usually steady enough to handle anything, began to tremble as I reached for them. Each movement felt magnified, clumsy. I stared at the arrangement, my breath catching in my throat, a dry, rasping sound. Who would send something like this? The silence of the room pressed in, amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest. I backed away slowly, my heel catching the edge of the table, and nearly knocked the whole thing over. A cold prickle crawled up my spine, making the fine hairs on my arms stand rigid. I needed to get out, needed to disappear before whatever this delivery represented decided to make itself known.

[story 2]

I found the package slipped under my door just as I was finishing my morning coffee. It was wrapped in thick, expensive paper, and when I peeled back the layers, the scent of lilies hit me—overpowering, cloying. Inside, a breathtaking bouquet. No inscription. Just blooms. My heart started hammering against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. I stood frozen in the hallway, clutching the box like it was a live wire. My vision narrowed, focusing only on the deep, unsettling green of the foliage. A low hum seemed to vibrate through the floorboards beneath my feet. Every instinct screamed at me to run, to bolt out the door and never look back. I swallowed hard, tasting bile at the back of my throat. My palms were slick with sudden sweat, and I could feel the blood draining from my face, leaving a clammy, awful sensation.

[story 3]

It arrived in the mail, unmarked save for a small, heavy box. When I finally pried the lid open, the sheer volume of the flowers overwhelmed me. They were lush, almost aggressively beautiful, a riot of yellow and purple that seemed too bright for my shadowed living room. I didn't touch them immediately; I just stood there, rooted to the spot, my gaze darting from petal to petal as if searching for a hidden trap. A strange tension coiled tight in my stomach, a sensation of profound wrongness. My throat constricted, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I could hear the distant sound of traffic, but it faded into a dull roar. I started pacing the small space, my movements jerky and uncontrolled, my eyes wide, scanning the empty room for any sign of who might have sent this silent, extravagant offering. The air felt thick, pressurized, as if something unseen was waiting just beyond the periphery of my sight.
